steps.title: Diagnosis & Examination
steps.surgeon_description: Clinical assessment begins with the Lachman test (most sensitive, performed at 20–30° flexion), the anterior drawer test, and the pivot-shift test to confirm ACL insufficiency and quantify rotational instability. Standard 4-view radiographs rule out bony avulsion, Segond fracture, and tibial plateau pathology. MRI at 1.5T or 3T confirms the diagnosis with >90% sensitivity and evaluates concomitant meniscal tears, chondral lesions, and bone bruising of the lateral femoral condyle and posterior lateral tibial plateau — the classic kissing contusion pattern of a non-contact ACL mechanism.
steps.patient_description: Your doctor examines your knee using specific hands-on tests — gently moving your leg in different directions — to check whether your ACL is torn and how unstable your knee feels. An MRI scan is then ordered to confirm the tear and to check for any other damage inside the knee, such as torn cartilage pads. Together, these findings guide your surgeon in planning exactly the right procedure for you.

steps.title: Anesthesia & Portal Placement
steps.surgeon_description: The patient is positioned supine with a lateral post and a well-leg holder, allowing the operative knee to hang freely at 90° flexion. After tourniquet inflation to 250–275 mmHg, the knee is prepped and draped. Standard anterolateral (AL) and anteromedial (AM) portals are established. The AL portal is created first using a spinal needle for localization, entering the joint just lateral to the patellar tendon and 1 cm above the joint line. The AM portal is placed under direct visualization through the AL portal, medial to the patellar tendon and superior to the medial meniscus, to allow unobstructed instrument access to the intercondylar notch. A superolateral outflow portal is optional.
steps.patient_description: You will receive anesthesia — either general (you are fully asleep) or spinal (you are awake but feel nothing from the waist down). Your surgical team positions your leg and makes two or three very small puncture incisions around the knee, each less than a centimeter wide. A tiny camera and instruments are passed through these openings — no large cuts are required.

steps.title: Diagnostic Arthroscopy
steps.surgeon_description: A systematic 11-point arthroscopic survey is performed: suprapatellar pouch, medial and lateral gutters, medial and lateral compartments (menisci, tibial and femoral cartilage surfaces), intercondylar notch (ACL stump, PCL), and the posterior compartments via the posteromedial and posterolateral portals as indicated. The ACL stump is probed to confirm complete rupture and assess stump vascularity for potential augmentation. Meniscal tears are classified by location (red-red, red-white, or white-white zones), pattern, and reparability. Any chondral defects are graded per the ICRS classification. Findings dictate whether concomitant meniscal repair or chondroplasty is performed at this stage.
steps.patient_description: With the camera inside your knee, your surgeon gets a complete picture of all the structures — the two cartilage pads (menisci), the joint surfaces, and the torn ACL. They carefully probe each structure to assess exactly what needs to be repaired. Any additional damage found is addressed at this point before moving on to the ACL reconstruction itself.

steps.title: Graft Harvesting
steps.surgeon_description: For hamstring autograft, a 3–4 cm oblique incision is made over the pes anserinus, 3–4 cm distal to the medial joint line, just medial to the tibial tubercle. The gracilis and semitendinosus tendons are identified, their distal pes attachments divided, and they are harvested with a closed tendon stripper advanced proximally until the musculotendinous junction is released. On the Arthrex graft preparation board, the tendons are whipstitched at each end using #2 FiberLoop suture, folded to a quadrupled (4-strand) construct, and tensioned at 20 lbs for 10 minutes. Final graft diameter is measured; a minimum 8 mm diameter is preferred for adequate tensile strength. The prepared graft is sized to the tunnel diameter and kept moist in a saline-soaked sponge.
steps.patient_description: The material for your new ACL — called a graft — is harvested from two small tendons behind your knee (the gracilis and semitendinosus). These tendons are folded into a thick four-strand bundle that is as strong as your original ligament. They naturally regrow over time, and their removal has minimal impact on knee strength. The graft is carefully prepared and sized on a specialized board before being implanted.

steps.title: Tibial Tunnel Drilling
steps.surgeon_description: The ACL stump is débrided with a shaver to expose the tibial footprint, preserving the PCL and posterolateral stump fibers as anatomic guides. An Arthrex tibial aiming device is placed arthroscopically with the hook positioned at the center of the native tibial footprint, approximately 7 mm anterior to the posterior cruciate ligament and midway between the tibial spines. The guide is set to 55° to establish the appropriate sagittal tunnel angle. A guide wire is drilled and its position confirmed arthroscopically and on fluoroscopy in the AP and lateral planes. The tunnel is sequentially reamed to match the graft diameter (typically 8–10 mm), and a notch-check is performed at 0° extension to confirm no roof impingement using the Howell technique.
steps.patient_description: Using a specialized aiming guide placed through the camera portal, your surgeon precisely marks the correct position in your shin bone. A guide wire is drilled first, and its position is confirmed under the camera before the tunnel is fully reamed. This tunnel creates the lower attachment point for your new ACL — its exact angle and location are critical to how the new ligament will function.

steps.title: Femoral Tunnel Drilling
steps.surgeon_description: The anatomic femoral footprint is identified at the 10–11 o'clock position (right knee) on the medial wall of the lateral femoral condyle, at the junction of the condylar cartilage and the posterior cortex (the resident's ridge). Using the Arthrex FlipCutter III through the tibial tunnel (transtibial) or via an accessory medial portal (the preferred anatomic technique at 100–120° of flexion), a guide wire is placed at the center of the AM bundle footprint. The FlipCutter is inserted over the guide wire and flipped to cutting position, then drilled retrograde to a depth of 25–30 mm to accommodate the femoral bone plug or cortical button device. A RetroButton or ACL TightRope RT is deployed through the cortex if cortical fixation is planned.
steps.patient_description: Your surgeon now creates a corresponding tunnel in the lower end of your thigh bone. Using a clever reversible drill — the Arthrex FlipCutter — inserted through the shin bone tunnel, the drill tip is flipped sideways to drill backward through the thigh bone from the inside out. This technique allows precise placement of the tunnel exactly where your natural ACL was attached.

steps.title: Graft Passage & Fixation
steps.surgeon_description: A passing suture or nitinol wire is looped through the femoral tunnel and retrieved through the tibial tunnel. The graft sutures are loaded and the graft is pulled into position from distal to proximal, confirming that the graft enters the femoral tunnel to the correct depth under arthroscopic visualization. Femoral fixation is achieved by deploying the RetroButton or TightRope RT cortical button against the lateral femoral cortex and tensioning the adjustable-loop construct. The knee is cycled 20 times through full range of motion to pre-tension the graft. Tibial fixation is then performed at 20° of flexion with the tibia in neutral rotation, using a PEEK or Bio-Tenodesis interference screw sized 1 mm larger than the tunnel. Final tension is applied to the graft sutures while the screw is advanced. Graft security and isometry are confirmed arthroscopically.
steps.patient_description: The prepared graft is passed through both tunnels using a suture shuttle, then secured with two specialized fixation devices — one at the thigh bone end (a cortical button called the TightRope) and one at the shin bone end (an interference screw). The knee is repeatedly flexed and extended to properly tension the graft before the final screws are tightened. The surgeon confirms inside the joint that the new ACL is taut, well-positioned, and does not catch on any bone surfaces.

steps.title: Final Assessment & Closure
steps.surgeon_title: Final Arthroscopic Assessment, Wound Closure & Rehabilitation Protocol
steps.patient_title: Completing the Surgery & Your Recovery Plan
steps.surgeon_description: Final arthroscopic inspection confirms graft isometry throughout range of motion, adequate notchplasty clearance at full extension, and absence of cyclops lesion risk. A manual Lachman test and pivot-shift are performed under anesthesia to confirm restoration of anterior stability. Portals are irrigated and closed with 3-0 Nylon or absorbable suture. An intra-articular drain may be placed per surgeon preference. Post-operatively, the patient is placed in a hinged knee brace locked in extension for ambulation, with immediate weight-bearing as tolerated. Structured rehabilitation begins within 24–48 hours: cryotherapy, quad sets, straight-leg raises, and patellar mobilization in the first week, with ROM progression, proprioceptive training, and sport-specific conditioning phased over 9–12 months before return-to-sport clearance.
steps.patient_description: Your surgeon takes a final look inside the joint to confirm that your new ACL is perfectly positioned, taut, and moves correctly as the knee bends and straightens. The small incisions are closed with a few stitches and covered with a dressing. You will wake up with your knee in a supportive brace. Your recovery begins the same day — a physiotherapist will guide you through gentle exercises within 24 to 48 hours, and most patients are walking with crutches within a day or two.
